# RedwoodSDK: Type-Safe Navigation with linkFor

## Background

A RedwoodSDK project is pre-installed at `/home/user/myapp`. Use `linkFor` from `rwsdk/router` to build a typed `link` helper, and use it inside server components so that every link in your HTML output is generated from the typed helper (no hard-coded path strings on the page).

## Requirements

Extend `src/worker.tsx` with the routes below and create a `src/app/shared/links.ts` file that exports a typed `link` constant created from `linkFor<App>()` (where `App` is the type of the default export from `src/worker.tsx`). Use that `link` helper inside the page components.

## Acceptance Criteria

* Project path: /home/user/myapp
* Start command: npm run dev -- --host 0.0.0.0 --port 5173
* Port: 5173
* Required file: `/home/user/myapp/src/app/shared/links.ts` must exist and export a value named `link`.
* Routes to implement (all under the existing `render(Document, ...)` block):
  * `GET /home`: server component HTML must contain an `<a>` element whose `href` is generated by calling `link("/about")` and whose visible text is `About`.
  * `GET /home`: server component HTML must also contain an `<a>` whose `href` is generated by calling `link("/users/:id", { id: "42" })` and whose visible text is `Show user 42`.
  * `GET /about`: HTML must contain an `<a>` whose `href` is generated by calling `link("/home")` and whose visible text is `Back to home`.
  * `GET /users/:id`: HTML must contain the text `User profile: <id>` (with the URL param substituted).
Let me start by exploring the project structure to understand the codebase.

export const Document: React.FC<{ children: React.ReactNode }> = ({
  children,
}) => (
  <html lang="en">
    <head>
      <meta charSet="utf-8" />
      <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
      <title>@redwoodjs/starter-minimal</title>
      <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
      <link
        rel="preconnect"
        href="https://fonts.gstatic.com"
        crossOrigin=""
      />
      <link
        rel="stylesheet"
        href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Noto+Sans:ital,wght@0,100..900;1,100..900&family=Playfair+Display:ital,wght@0,400..900;1,400..900&display=optional"
        precedence="first"
      />
      <link rel="modulepreload" href="/src/client.tsx" />
    </head>
    <body>
      {children}
      <script>import("/src/client.tsx")</script>
    </body>
  </html>
);

parts.text: Let me read the worker.d.ts to see the full `defineApp` and `AppDefinition` types:

output.content: File unchanged since last read. The content from the earlier Read tool_result in this conversation is still current — refer to that instead of re-reading.

parts.text: I now have a complete picture. Here are the findings:

input.result:
## Summary

The `rwsdk/router` module (exported from `./dist/runtime/entries/router.d.ts`) re-exports from two key files: `../lib/links` (which contains `linkFor`, `createLinks`, `defineLinks`, and related types) and `../lib/router` (which contains `route`, `index`, `prefix`, `layout`, `render`, `except`, `defineRoutes`, etc.).

---

## 1. What `linkFor` Accepts and Returns

**File:** `/home/user/myapp/node_modules/rwsdk/dist/runtime/lib/links.d.ts`

```typescript
export declare function linkFor<App>(): AppLink<App>;
```

- `linkFor` is a **generic function** that takes a **type parameter `App`** (not a runtime argument — it's called with zero arguments: `linkFor<MyApp>()`).
- It returns `AppLink<App>`, which resolves to `LinkFunction<AppRoutePaths<App>>`.

The type chain is:

```typescript
// Step 1: Extract route paths from the App type
type AppRoutes<App> = App extends { __rwRoutes: infer Routes } ? Routes : never;
type AppRoutePaths<App> = RoutePaths<AppRoutes<App>>;

// Step 2: The link function type
export type AppLink<App> = LinkFunction<AppRoutePaths<App>>;
```

`RoutePaths` recursively extracts path strings from `RouteDefinition` types and arrays:

```typescript
type RoutePaths<Value> =
  Value extends RouteDefinition<infer Path, any> ? Path
  : Value extends readonly (infer Item)[] ? RouteArrayPaths<Value>
  : Value extends RouteMiddleware<any> ? never
  : never;
```

So `linkFor<App>()` returns a `LinkFunction` parameterized on the union of all path strings defined in the app's routes.

---

## 2. The Returned `link` Function Signature

```typescript
export type LinkFunction<Paths extends string> = {
    <Path extends Paths>(path: Path, params?: ParamsForPath<Path>): string;
};
```

- **`path`**: Must be one of the string literal paths from the union `Paths` (derived from the app's routes). TypeScript will autocomplete/validate that the path exists.
- **`params`**: Optional. Its type is determined by `ParamsForPath<Path>`:
  - For **static paths** (e.g., `/about`): `params` is `undefined` (not required).
  - For **parameterized paths** (e.g., `/users/:id`): `params` is `{ id: string }`.
  - For **wildcard paths** (e.g., `/files/*`): `params` is `{ $0: string }`.
  - For **multiple wildcards** (e.g., `/docs/*/version/*`): `params` is `{ $0: string; $1: string }`.
- **Returns**: A `string` — the interpolated URL path with params substituted.

The `ParamsForPath` type:

```typescript
type PathParams<Path extends string> =
  Path extends `${string}:${infer Param}/${infer Rest}`
    ? { [K in Param]: string } & PathParams<Rest>
  : Path extends `${string}:${infer Param}`
    ? { [K in Param]: string }
  : Path extends `${string}*${infer Rest}`
    ? { $0: string } & PathParams<Rest>
  : Path extends `${string}*`
    ? { $0: string }
  : {};

type ParamsForPath<Path extends string> =
  PathParams<Path> extends Record<string, never> ? undefined : PathParams<Path>;
```

**Runtime behavior** (from `links.js`):
- If no params are provided but the route has parameters, it throws an error.
- If params are provided for a static route, it returns the path as-is.
- Parameter values are `encodeURIComponent`-encoded (wildcard segments are encoded individually, preserving `/` separators).
- If a param is missing or unused, it throws an error.

---

## 3. How `App` Type Should Be Used with `linkFor`

The `App` type must have a `__rwRoutes` property. This is produced by `defineApp`:

**File:** `/home/user/myapp/node_modules/rwsdk/dist/runtime/worker.d.ts`

```typescript
export type AppDefinition<Routes extends readonly Route<any>[], T extends RequestInfo> = {
    fetch: (request: Request, env: Env, cf: ExecutionContext) => Promise<Response>;
    __rwRoutes: Routes;
};

export declare const defineApp: <
    T extends RequestInfo = RequestInfo<any, DefaultAppContext>,
    Routes extends readonly Route<T>[] = readonly Route<T>[]
>(routes: Routes, options?: DefineAppOptions) => AppDefinition<Routes, T>;
```

**Usage pattern:**

```typescript
// In your worker.tsx:
import { defineApp, route, prefix, render } from "rwsdk/router";

export default defineApp([
  render(Document, [
    route("/", () => <HomePage />),
    route("/users/:id", ({ params }) => <UserPage id={params.id} />),
    route("/files/*", ({ params }) => <FilePage path={params.$0} />),
  ]),
]);

// Then elsewhere, to create a type-safe link function:
import { linkFor } from "rwsdk/router";
import type App from "./worker";  // or wherever defineApp is called

const link = linkFor<typeof App>();

// Usage:
link("/");                          // returns "/"
link("/users/:id", { id: "123" });  // returns "/users/123"
link("/files/*", { $0: "a/b/c" });  // returns "/files/a/b/c"
```

The `App` type is the **type of the default export** from the file that calls `defineApp()`. `defineApp` returns `AppDefinition<Routes, T>` which has the `__rwRoutes: Routes` property that `linkFor`'s type machinery depends on.

### Alternative: `defineLinks`

There's also `defineLinks` which has two overloads:

```typescript
// Overload 1: With App type (same as linkFor)
export declare function defineLinks<App extends { __rwRoutes: any }>(): AppLink<App>;

// Overload 2: With explicit route array
export declare function defineLinks<const T extends readonly string[]>(routes: T): LinkFunction<T[number]>;
```

The second overload lets you manually pass a route array for runtime validation:

```typescript
const link = defineLinks(["/", "/users/:id", "/files/*"]);
link("/users/:id", { id: "123" }); // works, and validates at runtime
```

---

## Key File Locations

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `/home/user/myapp/node_modules/rwsdk/dist/runtime/entries/router.d.ts` | Entry point for `rwsdk/router` — re-exports from links and router |
| `/home/user/myapp/node_modules/rwsdk/dist/runtime/lib/links.d.ts` | Type definitions for `linkFor`, `AppLink`, `LinkFunction`, `ParamsForPath`, etc. |
| `/home/user/myapp/node_modules/rwsdk/dist/runtime/lib/links.js` | Runtime implementation of `linkFor`, `createLinks`, `defineLinks` |
| `/home/user/myapp/node_modules/rwsdk/dist/runtime/lib/router.d.ts` | `route`, `RouteDefinition`, `RouteMiddleware`, `defineRoutes`, etc. |
| `/home/user/myapp/node_modules/rwsdk/dist/runtime/worker.d.ts` | `defineApp` and `AppDefinition` type (has `__rwRoutes`) |
| `/home/user/myapp/node_modules/rwsdk/dist/runtime/lib/types.d.ts` | `DocumentProps`, `LayoutProps`, `RwContext` |
| `/home/user/myapp/node_modules/rwsdk/package.json` | Export map — `"./router"` → `router.d.ts` (types) and `routerClient.js` (default) |

## Summary

The `rwsdk/router` module (exported from `./dist/runtime/entries/router.d.ts`) re-exports from two key files: `../lib/links` (which contains `linkFor`, `createLinks`, `defineLinks`, and related types) and `../lib/router` (which contains `route`, `index`, `prefix`, `layout`, `render`, `except`, `defineRoutes`, etc.).

---

## 1. What `linkFor` Accepts and Returns

**File:** `/home/user/myapp/node_modules/rwsdk/dist/runtime/lib/links.d.ts`

```typescript
export declare function linkFor<App>(): AppLink<App>;
```

- `linkFor` is a **generic function** that takes a **type parameter `App`** (not a runtime argument — it's called with zero arguments: `linkFor<MyApp>()`).
- It returns `AppLink<App>`, which resolves to `LinkFunction<AppRoutePaths<App>>`.

The type chain is:

```typescript
// Step 1: Extract route paths from the App type
type AppRoutes<App> = App extends { __rwRoutes: infer Routes } ? Routes : never;
type AppRoutePaths<App> = RoutePaths<AppRoutes<App>>;

// Step 2: The link function type
export type AppLink<App> = LinkFunction<AppRoutePaths<App>>;
```

`RoutePaths` recursively extracts path strings from `RouteDefinition` types and arrays:

```typescript
type RoutePaths<Value> =
  Value extends RouteDefinition<infer Path, any> ? Path
  : Value extends readonly (infer Item)[] ? RouteArrayPaths<Value>
  : Value extends RouteMiddleware<any> ? never
  : never;
```

So `linkFor<App>()` returns a `LinkFunction` parameterized on the union of all path strings defined in the app's routes.

---

## 2. The Returned `link` Function Signature

```typescript
export type LinkFunction<Paths extends string> = {
    <Path extends Paths>(path: Path, params?: ParamsForPath<Path>): string;
};
```

- **`path`**: Must be one of the string literal paths from the union `Paths` (derived from the app's routes). TypeScript will autocomplete/validate that the path exists.
- **`params`**: Optional. Its type is determined by `ParamsForPath<Path>`:
  - For **static paths** (e.g., `/about`): `params` is `undefined` (not required).
  - For **parameterized paths** (e.g., `/users/:id`): `params` is `{ id: string }`.
  - For **wildcard paths** (e.g., `/files/*`): `params` is `{ $0: string }`.
  - For **multiple wildcards** (e.g., `/docs/*/version/*`): `params` is `{ $0: string; $1: string }`.
- **Returns**: A `string` — the interpolated URL path with params substituted.

The `ParamsForPath` type:

```typescript
type PathParams<Path extends string> =
  Path extends `${string}:${infer Param}/${infer Rest}`
    ? { [K in Param]: string } & PathParams<Rest>
  : Path extends `${string}:${infer Param}`
    ? { [K in Param]: string }
  : Path extends `${string}*${infer Rest}`
    ? { $0: string } & PathParams<Rest>
  : Path extends `${string}*`
    ? { $0: string }
  : {};

type ParamsForPath<Path extends string> =
  PathParams<Path> extends Record<string, never> ? undefined : PathParams<Path>;
```

**Runtime behavior** (from `links.js`):
- If no params are provided but the route has parameters, it throws an error.
- If params are provided for a static route, it returns the path as-is.
- Parameter values are `encodeURIComponent`-encoded (wildcard segments are encoded individually, preserving `/` separators).
- If a param is missing or unused, it throws an error.

---

## 3. How `App` Type Should Be Used with `linkFor`

The `App` type must have a `__rwRoutes` property. This is produced by `defineApp`:

**File:** `/home/user/myapp/node_modules/rwsdk/dist/runtime/worker.d.ts`

```typescript
export type AppDefinition<Routes extends readonly Route<any>[], T extends RequestInfo> = {
    fetch: (request: Request, env: Env, cf: ExecutionContext) => Promise<Response>;
    __rwRoutes: Routes;
};

export declare const defineApp: <
    T extends RequestInfo = RequestInfo<any, DefaultAppContext>,
    Routes extends readonly Route<T>[] = readonly Route<T>[]
>(routes: Routes, options?: DefineAppOptions) => AppDefinition<Routes, T>;
```

**Usage pattern:**

```typescript
// In your worker.tsx:
import { defineApp, route, prefix, render } from "rwsdk/router";

export default defineApp([
  render(Document, [
    route("/", () => <HomePage />),
    route("/users/:id", ({ params }) => <UserPage id={params.id} />),
    route("/files/*", ({ params }) => <FilePage path={params.$0} />),
  ]),
]);

// Then elsewhere, to create a type-safe link function:
import { linkFor } from "rwsdk/router";
import type App from "./worker";  // or wherever defineApp is called

const link = linkFor<typeof App>();

// Usage:
link("/");                          // returns "/"
link("/users/:id", { id: "123" });  // returns "/users/123"
link("/files/*", { $0: "a/b/c" });  // returns "/files/a/b/c"
```

The `App` type is the **type of the default export** from the file that calls `defineApp()`. `defineApp` returns `AppDefinition<Routes, T>` which has the `__rwRoutes: Routes` property that `linkFor`'s type machinery depends on.

### Alternative: `defineLinks`

There's also `defineLinks` which has two overloads:

```typescript
// Overload 1: With App type (same as linkFor)
export declare function defineLinks<App extends { __rwRoutes: any }>(): AppLink<App>;

// Overload 2: With explicit route array
export declare function defineLinks<const T extends readonly string[]>(routes: T): LinkFunction<T[number]>;
```

The second overload lets you manually pass a route array for runtime validation:

```typescript
const link = defineLinks(["/", "/users/:id", "/files/*"]);
link("/users/:id", { id: "123" }); // works, and validates at runtime
```

---

## Key File Locations

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `/home/user/myapp/node_modules/rwsdk/dist/runtime/entries/router.d.ts` | Entry point for `rwsdk/router` — re-exports from links and router |
| `/home/user/myapp/node_modules/rwsdk/dist/runtime/lib/links.d.ts` | Type definitions for `linkFor`, `AppLink`, `LinkFunction`, `ParamsForPath`, etc. |
| `/home/user/myapp/node_modules/rwsdk/dist/runtime/lib/links.js` | Runtime implementation of `linkFor`, `createLinks`, `defineLinks` |
| `/home/user/myapp/node_modules/rwsdk/dist/runtime/lib/router.d.ts` | `route`, `RouteDefinition`, `RouteMiddleware`, `defineRoutes`, etc. |
| `/home/user/myapp/node_modules/rwsdk/dist/runtime/worker.d.ts` | `defineApp` and `AppDefinition` type (has `__rwRoutes`) |
| `/home/user/myapp/node_modules/rwsdk/dist/runtime/lib/types.d.ts` | `DocumentProps`, `LayoutProps`, `RwContext` |
| `/home/user/myapp/node_modules/rwsdk/package.json` | Export map — `"./router"` → `router.d.ts` (types) and `routerClient.js` (default) |

Now I have a clear understanding. The key insight is that `App` needs to be the type of the default export from `worker.tsx`, which is the return type of `defineApp(...)`. The `links.ts` file already exists but uses `import type { App } from "rwsdk/worker"` — this is incorrect. It should use `typeof` the default export from `worker.tsx`.

input.result: All acceptance criteria are met. Here's a summary of what was implemented: ### Files created/modified: **`src/app/shared/links.ts`** — Fixed to import the `App` type from `@/worker` (the default export of `worker.tsx`) and use `linkFor()` to create a type-safe `link` helper. **`src/worker.tsx`** — Extended with three new routes: - `route("/home", Home)` — `/home` route - `route("/about", About)` — `/about` route - `route("/users/:id", User)` — `/users/:id` parameterized route **`src/app/pages/home.tsx`** — Updated to use the `link` helper: - `About` - `Show user 42` **`src/app/pages/about.tsx`** (new) — About page with: - `Back to home` **`src/app/pages/user.tsx`** (new) — User profile page displaying `User profile: {id}` with the URL param substituted. All links are type-safe — the `link` function provides autocomplete for valid paths and enforces correct params at compile time via TypeScript. The project builds successfully and all routes serve the expected HTML.
